title: WhatsApp Audio 2026-03-25 at 12.11.25 PM.mp3
content:
# TRANSCRIPT
 We are discussing about sequential models. Sequential models mainly used for sequential data, maybe a text, maybe a voice, maybe a time series type of data. In that we have seen the very first architecture that is the very first architecture that is RNNs. RNNs are supposed to be simplest architectures which can deal with the serial, text or voice kind of data. How is the structure of a cell? Simply you are taking the output of hidden layer and you are fitting it back as a feedback layer. So H is nothing but output of hidden layer at previous timestamp and that's why HT-1 is the representation. So your present output which is ht is dependent on current input xt and previous output ht-1. And simply we were applying these two things in a neural network which contains a tanh activation function. And that's it. This is the simplest RMM cell representation. What was the problem with this? The problem is these RNNs cannot deal with the long sentences. For long sentences we need a lockdown memory. Because when we keep on adding these RNN hidden layers one after another and when we are adding a huge number of hidden layers in between, vanishing granule descent will come into picture. As a result of which the effect of earlier words will be keep on fading as you are going to the longer sentences. And then the idea of LSTM came into picture. So LSTM, what is the core idea? Now they are saying that we will maintain two states instead of one state, hidden state, we will be having two states. One is a cell state, cell state represented by C and we will be having a hidden state as well as we were having in RLNs. So this will work as a short term memory and this cell state will work as a long term memory and hence collectively you can call it as long short term memory unit lstf architectures in this architecture we have already discussed the three main gates are there so what you are having is You are having forget gate. What we are doing in this forget gate? We are taking the current input XT. we are taking the current input XT, we are taking previous hidden state output and we are taking previous long term memory as well and what we are doing we are feeding current input and previous output hidden layer just like RNN combining these two in a and feeding in a neural network but now this neural network is containing a sigmoid activation function. Output of this which we call as empty per gate gate's output and input from the long Long term memory, what we are doing here? We are performing point wise multiplication operation we are performing and this is the task of forget game. After this we are going to the next gate and this gate acts as an input gate. Now what we are doing here? Again we are taking XT and HP. We are feeding it just like we did in a forget gate. we are feeding them here in a neural network with sigmoid activation function output of that we can call it as say IT input gates output see FT IT we are taking here and we and we are also fitting it to the neural network with TANH and this will give me ic Why we are using term called c is the candidate C represents candidate. So possible candidate input we are talking about. And again we are performing bitwise multiplication and we are adding it with this bitwise addition operation will be performed whatever is coming from this output is forget gates output and the previous long term memory output point wise quantification output of that and output from this side will be done point wise addition and this is what we are exactly calculating what is suppose to be forget and what is suppose to be added in the long term memory and this is working as a input gate. After this, the third and final gate of LSTM, this gate is known as output gate. What operations we are doing? Again, XT and HT minus 1, we are feeding to the neural network with activation function sigmoid output of that we are taking here and from this side whatever was the output from the input gate we are feeding it from this side and we are applying tanh operation on this tanh this tanh operation is also point point wise operation. Yes. Result of that result of that damage operation will be point wise multiplied with the output from this side and whatever we are getting this will be considered as a ht that is current output and whatever we were getting at this side will be considered as a ct which is long term output of a current state. and as and when it is a final state this can be taken out as a final h t if it is not final h t it will go in a loop and whatever number of layers you are having for all that layers this will repeat its process Are we clear with LSTM Yes So RNN and LSTMs LSTMs are overcoming the problem which was there in the RNNs. They are now able to deal with long term memories. So it can deal with the long sentences. So problem of vanishing gradually we say, forgetting about the earlier words will not happen in this field. The point to be remembered here is size of all these neural networks needs to be same. and this thing we have discussed in last lecture. Why it is required? Because the output that is generated from all these operations needs to be uniform if we have to perform these point wise operations. This was entire discussion that we did for LSPM and then we discussed discussion that we did for lsdm and then we discussed what is the problem with this what is the issue with this architecture actually there is no issue with the architecture but people find it complex and computational power required for this lsdm unit is much more and wherever we are having a constrained devices what do you mean by constrained devices devices which operates on a battery devices which are having a limited computational power especially when we are dealing with the iot devices okay we cannot afford the LSTM units that is why something lightweight alternative is required and that alternative is GRU that is gated recurrent unit. Now if we compare LSTMs with GRUs performance is almost same I am saying almost same of course LSTM are having upper hand as they are superior but LRU this GRU will be okay if you are having some restrictions let us see what is done in GRU but before going further you must understand these two things are we clear about all these all these things then GRU will be very very easy to understand.

***
# NOTES
**Lecture Title: Introduction to Sequential Models and LSTM**

🔹 **Key Topics:**
- Sequential Models
- RNNs (Recurrent Neural Networks)
- LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) Architecture
- GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)

🔹 **Important Definitions:**
- **RNNs**: A type of neural network used for sequential data, such as text or voice.
- **LSTM**: A type of RNN architecture that uses a cell state to maintain long-term memory.
- **GRU**: A lightweight alternative to LSTM, used for sequential data.

🔹 **Detailed Notes:**

* **RNNs**: RNNs are used for sequential data and have a simple architecture. The output of the hidden layer is fed back as a feedback layer, creating a loop.
* **RNN Cell Structure**: The RNN cell takes the output of the hidden layer (H) and the input (X) and uses a tanh activation function to produce the output (Ht).
* **Problem with RNNs**: RNNs cannot handle long sentences due to the vanishing gradient problem, where the effect of earlier words fades as the sentence length increases.
* **LSTM Architecture**: LSTM uses two states: a cell state (C) and a hidden state (H). The cell state acts as a long-term memory, while the hidden state acts as a short-term memory.
* **LSTM Gates**: LSTM has three gates: the forget gate, the input gate, and the output gate.
	+ **Forget Gate**: The forget gate decides what to discard from the cell state. It takes the current input (Xt), previous hidden state (Ht-1), and previous cell state (Ct-1) and uses a sigmoid activation function to produce the forget gate output.
	+ **Input Gate**: The input gate decides what new information to add to the cell state. It takes the current input (Xt), previous hidden state (Ht-1), and previous cell state (Ct-1) and uses a sigmoid activation function to produce the input gate output.
	+ **Output Gate**: The output gate decides what information to output from the cell state. It takes the current input (Xt), previous hidden state (Ht-1), and previous cell state (Ct-1) and uses a sigmoid activation function to produce the output gate output.
* **GRU**: GRU is a lightweight alternative to LSTM, used for sequential data. It has a similar architecture to LSTM but with fewer gates.

🔹 **Examples Discussed:**
- The lecture discusses the RNN cell structure and the problem with RNNs.
- The lecture explains the LSTM architecture and its gates.
- The lecture introduces the GRU and its similarities to LSTM.

🔹 **Summary:**
The lecture introduces sequential models and the RNN architecture. It explains the problem with RNNs and introduces the LSTM architecture as a solution. The LSTM architecture uses a cell state to maintain long-term memory and has three gates: the forget gate, the input gate, and the output gate. The lecture also introduces the GRU as a lightweight alternative to LSTM.

🔹 **Possible Exam Questions:**
1. What is the main problem with RNNs?
2. What is the cell state in LSTM, and how does it work?
3. What are the three gates in LSTM, and what do they do?
4. How does GRU differ from LSTM?
5. What are the advantages and disadvantages of using LSTM and GRU?

***
# NOTES
**Lecture Title:** Sequential Models: RNNs, LSTMs, and GRUs

**Key Topics:**
- RNNs (Recurrent Neural Networks)
- LSTMs (Long Short-Term Memory)
- GRUs (Gated Recurrent Units)

**Important Definitions:**
- **RNN**: A type of neural network used for sequential data, such as text, voice, or time series data.
- **LSTM**: A type of RNN that uses a memory cell to store information for long periods of time.
- **GRU**: A type of RNN that is a lightweight alternative to LSTMs.
- **Vanishing Gradient Problem**: A problem in RNNs where the gradients of the loss function become very small, making it difficult to train the network.
- **Long Term Memory**: A type of memory that can store information for long periods of time.
- **Short Term Memory**: A type of memory that can store information for short periods of time.

**Detailed Notes (Bullet Points):**

* RNNs are used for sequential data and have a simple architecture that can deal with serial data.
* The structure of an RNN cell consists of a hidden layer and a feedback layer, where the output of the hidden layer is fed back as input.
* RNNs have a problem with dealing with long sentences, as the gradients of the loss function become very small, making it difficult to train the network.
* LSTMs were introduced to overcome the problem of RNNs, by using a memory cell to store information for long periods of time.
* LSTMs have three main gates: the forget gate, the input gate, and the output gate.
* The forget gate takes the current input, the previous hidden state, and the previous long term memory, and outputs a value that determines how much of the previous long term memory to forget.
* The input gate takes the current input, the previous hidden state, and the previous long term memory, and outputs a value that determines how much new information to add to the long term memory.
* The output gate takes the current input, the previous hidden state, and the previous long term memory, and outputs a value that determines the output of the LSTM.
* GRUs are a lightweight alternative to LSTMs, with a simpler architecture that still performs well.
* GRUs have two main gates: the update gate and the reset gate.
* The update gate determines how much of the previous hidden state to update, while the reset gate determines how much of the previous hidden state to reset.

**Examples Discussed:**
- The example of using LSTMs to deal with long sentences was discussed.
- The example of using GRUs as a lightweight alternative to LSTMs was discussed.

**Summary:**
In this lecture, we discussed the basics of sequential models, including RNNs, LSTMs, and GRUs. We saw how RNNs have a problem with dealing with long sentences, and how LSTMs were introduced to overcome this problem. We also saw how GRUs are a lightweight alternative to LSTMs, with a simpler architecture that still performs well. We discussed the architecture of LSTMs, including the forget gate, the input gate, and the output gate, and how GRUs have two main gates: the update gate and the reset gate.

**Possible Exam Questions:**

1. What is the main problem with RNNs when dealing with long sentences?
2. What is the architecture of an LSTM cell?
3. What are the three main gates in an LSTM?
4. What is the purpose of the forget gate in an LSTM?
5. What is the purpose of the input gate in an LSTM?
6. What is the purpose of the output gate in an LSTM?
7. What is the main difference between LSTMs and GRUs?
8. What is the architecture of a GRU cell?
